concurrency.angle: SYNTHESIS: AM Brain v3 + Jack Storage/Sync Architecture for Meta APAC Account Manager (33 accounts, single operator)

findings.claim: AM Brain REV3 canonical store = Metamate project memory; host = Metamate Automations; GDrive markdown+git = one-way read-only backup
findings.evidence: AM Brain canonical spec (2026-06-09, §11 REV3): 'Canonical store = Metamate project memory (/memories/project/<id>/); Cron (Automation/Sandcastle) and operator (Collab Files in the Mac browser) read/write the same namespace. One store, two surfaces → no sync, no drift.' Eliminates concurrency at the cost of dependency on Metamate Automations reliability.

findings.claim: Jack's bidirectional sync (GDrive primary, MM2 secondary, 30-min cycle, LLM additive merge) is production-proven but carries silent data loss risk from LWW during sync window
findings.evidence: Jack manual (§10, §16): GDrive wins on conflict, but sync runs every 30 min. If both agents edit context.md between T=0:00 and T=0:30, sync at T=0:30 applies LWW: 'Timeline example: T=0:00 Metamate updates context.md, T=0:15 Claude Code updates same file, T=0:30 Sync runs → CONFLICT → GDrive version kept, Metamate's T=0:00 changes LOST unless LLM merge recovers them.' Web evidence (Stacksync): LWW produces no warnings; lost updates are silent.

findings.claim: Additive merge (keeping ALL info from both sides) cannot reliably recover information that LWW deleted if merge LLM misses it
findings.evidence: Jack's merge rule (§10): 'LLM additive merge — keep ALL information from both versions, deduplicate.' But this is a detection problem, not a prevention problem. If the merge LLM doesn't spot that Metamate's T=0:00 edit was lost, it stays lost. Web evidence (ResearchGate): Knowledge base merging causes 'loss of weighted information in the resulting KB after merging' and F-measure quantifies information loss during merge.

findings.claim: AM Brain's single-writer + append-only architecture prevents silent data loss at cost of no unattended scheduling if devserver is unavailable
findings.evidence: AM Brain I-7 & §11 REV3: 'One canonical store; no bidirectional merge.' With Metamate Automations: hosted + fires laptop-closed. With devserver (REV2): 'persistent Meta devserver, not laptop...runs natively on a Meta devserver.' But §11 VERIFY item 2 (blocked): 'No self-serve persistent devserver from Mac — ondemand broker = ephemeral OD only.'

findings.claim: Metamate Automations is architecturally incompatible with file+git canonical if code must be delivered via skillbook/scmquery/Dataswarm, not arbitrary Python
findings.evidence: AM Brain §11 REV3 finding: 'Automations...cannot host our current design: (a) Drive access is connector/API by Google file ID, NOT a FUSE mount...(b) raw .md files are not reliably readable...(c) no git over the Drive-backed vault...(d) arbitrary python3 -m engine.pipeline is not native — code must be delivered via skillbook cells, fbsource source: scmquery, or Dataswarm PythonMetamateAgentOperator.'

findings.claim: Single-writer pattern achieves strong consistency and prevents race conditions; multi-writer requires complex conflict resolution or loses data silently
findings.evidence: Web evidence (Medium/Orleans): Single-writer/multiple-reader ensures 'strict serializability, no aborts, wait-freedom with strong bounds on step complexity, precise garbage collection.' Multi-writer 'provides write scale-out...but requires complex conflict detection mechanisms at log level.' For solo AM: single-writer is overkill on complexity avoided.

findings.claim: Jack's per-file file-locking protocol (atomic mkdir for pessimistic locks, 10-min stale timeout) is production-tested and works across agents
findings.evidence: Jack §15: 'mkdir is atomic on POSIX. Success = you own it. Failure = someone else has it. If <10min old → wait/retry; >10min old → stale (agent crashed), rmdir and retry.' No data loss reported in Jack's 14-skill, 34-account production system.

findings.claim: Metamate project memory API's optimistic locking (content_sha256 preconditions) is native, requires no application-level file locking
findings.evidence: Claude API docs: 'To avoid clobbering concurrent write, pass content_sha256 precondition. Update only applies if stored content hash matches what was read; on mismatch, re-read and retry.' Every mutation creates immutable memver_... version. Built-in, no filesystem tricks needed.

findings.claim: Append-only ledgers (interactions/, commitments/, rs-ledger history, audit.log) eliminate overwrites but require explicit tombstone entries for correction
findings.evidence: AM Brain DM-10 & §7.4: 'A retracted fact gets a tombstone entry (not_true: <claim>; do_not_rederive: true) which MUST suppress re-derivation from old source material.' SPEC §9/ALG-8: 'append-only; never edit past entries.' Correction protocol: 'An AM edit is ground truth; derived stores re-sync. A wrong memory gets tombstone so re-ingestion cannot resurrect it.'

findings.claim: Two-tier dossier (mirror tier = regenerable cache; authored tier = durable truth) avoids sync conflicts by never syncing back the mirror
findings.evidence: AM Brain §4: 'MIRROR tier (thin caches; fetched_at; freely deletable; NOT git/backup)' vs 'AUTHORED tier (durable truth; git-versioned)'. No bidirectional merge because 'mirror tier always carries fetched_at; never source of truth; not versioned/backed up; query-live-first.'

recommended_patterns: RULING: For ship-now (Phase 2 + onward): Use AM Brain REV3 **only if Metamate Automations reliability is validated** (operator health-check: weekly cron runs >99% + <5min latency). Single canonical store (Metamate project memory), one-way nightly backup (GDrive + git, read-only, never synced back), operator edits via Collab Files in browser. No bidirectional sync, no merge conflict. [source: AM Brain REV3, validated vs Stacksync LWW risk]

FALLBACK: If Automations unreliable or unavailable: Use **persistent devserver (REV2)** with GDrive+git canonical mounted via mclone+systemd. Requires separate devserver provisioning request (currently blocked). Keeps I-7 intact, zero Metamate dependency. Still requires per-file single-writer (one skill owns one section) to avoid concurrent edits. [source: AM Brain §11 REV2, Jack's ownership-matrix]

HYBRID (highest safety for early maturity): Metamate memory canonical + Collab Files operator edits + **manual laptop runs as fallback trigger**. Operator runs `/today` cockpit manually each morning instead of relying on Automations cron. Kills the 'unattended' property but retains all data-consistency wins of single-writer canonical. Viable for 3-4 month maturity ramp while Automations reliability is proven. [source: AM Brain option 3 recommendation §11]

NEVER: Do NOT use Jack's bidirectional sync (30-min cycle, LLM additive merge, GDrive primary) unless operator can tolerate silent information loss during the sync window. Production-proven ≠ safe for this team. Jack's system works because Jack actively uses it daily (catches errors fast); Jason's usage is async (batch update), sync conflicts more likely to hide. [source: Web evidence (Stacksync): 'Without strategy, you risk data loss or infinite sync loops.' § Jack §10 Timeline example: Metamate changes LOST.]

STORE ARCHITECTURE: Adopt AM Brain's two-tier dossier everywhere: (1) MIRROR tier = performance.md, rs-ledger (generated daily from data plane), interactions/<YYYY-MM>.md (CI digest), contacts.md (PII-minimal). These are regenerable, carry fetched_at, NOT git-backed up, query-live-first. (2) AUTHORED tier = profile.md, relationship.md, narrative.md, commitments.md, style.md, all 8 strategy/relationship/voice files. These are durable, git-versioned, human-owned or AM-confirmed, appendable only. [source: AM Brain §4, Jack's narrative dual-load §17]

WRITE COORDINATION: Implement per-file single-writer ownership, NOT per-session taint. One skill (e.g., /refresh, /post-meeting, /calendar) is the owner of each section. Other skills can only PROPOSE diffs or append to ledgers. No silent overwrites. Use frontmatter 'owner: skill-name' to enforce at ingestion. [source: AM Brain I-5, Jack §12 ownership-matrix]

CONFLICT FALLBACK (if file+git canonical is chosen): Use Jack's atomic mkdir file-locking protocol (pessimistic lock, 10-min stale timeout) before any agent writes. No need for Metamate optimistic preconditions if locking is application-level. Proven production pattern. [source: Jack §15]

CONCURRENCY FOR METAMATE MEMORY: If using Metamate project memory canonical, leverage built-in optimistic locking: always read with content_sha256, always retry on mismatch. Do NOT implement application-level file locking on top of it (adds complexity, memory API already handles). Let platform handle the retry loop. [source: Claude API docs, Metamate memory]

APPEND-ONLY DISCIPLINE: Enforce strict append-only for interactions/raw/<YYYY-MM-DD>-<type>.md, commitments.md, rs-ledger history, audit.log. Corrections MUST be new tombstone entries ('not_true: <previous claim>; do_not_rederive: true'), never edits to past entries. Encode this in templates + code review gate (agent diffs must show append, never mutation). [source: AM Brain DM-10, §7.4, SPEC §9 ALG-8]

METADATA VALIDATION AT INGESTION: Every generated claim carries source + fetched_at + clearance class. Enforce DM-4 rule ('missing data ≠ zero') before any merge or ranking decision. Cache diff (Tier 2 filter) catches stale performance before Tier 3 (LLM) wakes. No merge of unvalidated numerics. [source: AM Brain I-3, I-8, DM-4, §7]

NARRATIVE DUAL-LOAD (NOT BIDIRECTIONAL MERGE): narrative.md is append-only reverse-chron meeting log (updated by 96h transcript sweep + post-meeting capture). Never edit past entries. No sync back from MM2 to GDrive for this file—it's only read-only retrieval. Store it in both places (canonical in Metamate memory + read-only copy in GDrive for history), but updates only happen in canonical. [source: AM Brain §4, Jack §17 narrative, SPEC §7.4]

VERSION MANAGEMENT: Use git (if file-based) only for recovery/history, not as the live source-of-truth under concurrent writes. Commit after every write (audit trail), but treat .git as a recovery layer, not a conflict-resolution mechanism. If Metamate memory is canonical, .git is nightly one-way backup only. [source: AM Brain I-7, REV3 §11]

TESTING GATE: Before going live, run a concurrency test (two agents simulate conflict window, verify no silent data loss). If using Metamate memory: verify content_sha256 retries actually work. If using file+git: verify mkdir lock timeout catches stale locks. Do NOT deploy based on theory alone. [source: AM Brain §13 conformance, Web evidence: LWW failures are silent until you test]
concurrency.implications:
**CHOICE HIERARCHY FOR JASON'S 33-ACCOUNT APAC SYSTEM:** (1) **AM Brain REV3 (Metamate memory canonical)** is the longest-term, simplest architecture IF Automations can be validated stable. No sync complexity, no merge LLM risk, native optimistic locking. Trade-off: depends on Metamate Automations reliability (currently has known ~23-mission silent-stop bug, intermittent missed runs). Best if operator is willing to do a 2-week stability prove-out. (2) **AM Brain REV2 (GDrive+git canonical on persistent devserver)** is the safest if a devserver can be provisioned. Proven cron scheduler, same file+git storage Jason already uses in am-brain-v1, zero Metamate dependency. Trade-off: requires separate provisioning request (currently blocked on operator's side). Viable if devserver is available within 1-2 weeks. (3) **AM Brain option 3 (Metamate memory + manual laptop runs)** is the pragmatic bridge: single-writer canonical (data consistency), operator triggers `/today` manually each morning (no unattended property, but acceptable for ramp), keeps operator in the loop (catches issues fast). Viable immediately, can graduate to cron once Automations proven. (4) **NEVER Jack's bidirectional sync** for this case. Jack's system works because Jack uses it actively daily; Jason's usage is likely async/batch (nightly review of cockpit). Sync conflicts are more likely to hide information loss, and the 30-minute window means critical edits can disappear without warning. Production-proven ≠ safe for this operational model. **RECOMMENDATION FOR 2026-06-09 FORWARD:** Start with option 3 (Metamate memory canonical + manual laptop runs). Ship the core (L1 knowledge store, L2 data plane, L4 decision engine, L5 gate) with single-writer discipline and append-only ledgers. In parallel, (a) validate Metamate Automations reliability (health-check 2 weeks), (b) request persistent devserver (contingency, 2-4 weeks). Graduate to unattended cron once either path is proven. This preserves data integrity (single-writer, no merge), respects the solo AM's operational reality (one person, async usage patterns), and gives infrastructure time to stabilize. Avoid the siren call of \"synced systems are agile\"—they're not, when information loss is silent.",
"**STORAGE/SYNC DECISION RULING:** Single-writer + append-only beats bidirectional+merge for a solo AM's knowledge store. The reason: in a 33-account portfolio with ~400 open RS and monthly CI pacing, silent information loss (from LWW or failed merge detection) is catastrophic—it means the AM acts on stale/wrong data without knowing. Jack's system handles this because Jack's daily engagement catches deltas fast (\"did my post-meeting capture get lost?\"). Jason's overnight/async usage means losses can hide for hours or days. Therefore: (1) Canonical store = single location (Metamate memory OR GDrive+git mounted, not both). (2) Writes go through one orchestrator + per-file owned skills, no concurrent mutations. (3) All changes are append-only or explicit tombstones, never silent overwrites. (4) Backup is one-way (for DR + history), never synced back. (5) Conflict resolution is \"don't create them\" (serialization), not \"detect them after\" (merge).",
"**OPERATIONAL IMPLICATION FOR JACK'S SYSTEM:** If Jack continues using his bidirectional setup, he should add: (a) mandatory weekly audit of sync conflicts (explicit report: 'Conflicts resolved by LLM merge: N; Instances where merge recovered data that would have been lost: M'). (b) Versioning on context.md snapshots every sync (so rollback is possible if merge is wrong). (c) A sanity check: spot-sample 2-3 merged context.md files per week against the raw Presto/CRM to catch if merge LLM missed critical updates. Jack's production experience is valuable, but it doesn't transfer directly to async usage patterns. The risk surface is different."]
caveats: Metamate Automations reliability (23-mission silent-stop bug, ~5% missed-run rate) is from operator intake notes (am-brain-canonical.md §11 VERIFY item 3), not published SLAs or third-party testing. Must be independently validated before committing to it as the unattended scheduler. Without validation, option 3 (manual laptop runs) is the safer starting point.

Persistent devserver provisioning is currently blocked (§11 VERIFY item 2: 'No self-serve persistent devserver from Mac; ondemand broker = ephemeral OD only'). Requires operator to file separate request. Timeline unknown. REV2 path cannot be chosen unilaterally by the agent.

Metamate Automations architectural incompatibility with file+git (no FUSE mount, .md files not reliably readable, no git, arbitrary Python not native) means switching to Automations would require reopening I-7 (move canonical state to Sheet/Hive, land engine/ in fbsource, drop git). This is a deliberate redesign, not a drop-in. REV3 and REV2 are mutually exclusive strategies, not fork points.

Jack's production system has been in operation for multiple months with real clients, but concurrency stress-test results are not published. We know 'no data loss reported,' but this is absence-of-evidence, not evidence-of-absence. The two-writer contention window (30-min sync) suggests conflicts are statistically rare (single AM, single active call per time zone), but operational risk is nonzero.

Append-only+tombstone discipline requires operator behavior enforcement. If Jason manually edits a past interaction record (violating append-only), the system cannot prevent it. Code review gate + templates can encourage correct behavior, but human discipline is the ultimate enforcement. No technical guarantee.

Knowledge base merging loss quantification (F-measure from academic literature) is theoretical. No evidence found for how often the AM Brain's specific merge strategy (LLM additive merge, "keep ALL info from both versions") actually loses information in practice. Empirical failure rate is unknown. Test with realistic conflict scenarios before committing.

Single-writer pattern assumed to be sufficient for 33 accounts + 2-3 concurrent agents (Claude Code, Metamate, Codex). At 5+ agents or >100 accounts, serialization bottleneck may emerge. No guidance found for scaling single-writer to higher concurrency without converting to multi-writer (which reintroduces all the conflicts).

Google Drive mclone+git inside FUSE mount (REV2) has not been tested on an actual persistent Meta devserver in this project. Prior art (gdrive-mount skill) is assumed to work, but is a live risk for REV2 path. Should be tested on a trial devserver before banking Phase 3+ on it.

Web evidence on CRDTs (concurrent text insertion garbling) is worst-case. Simpler CRDTs (counters, OR-sets, presence sets) are safe. Applicability to AM prose files is limited; CRDTs are not recommended for narrative.md or strategy fields, but could be useful for numeric fields (total_ci_count, rs_closed_count). Not explored in this synthesis.

pkm-compounding.angle: Single-canonical-store compiled-wiki (AM Brain REV3) vs bidirectional-merge additive-sync (Jack): Which storage/sync pattern compounds knowledge better, loses less data, and scales for semi-autonomous 30+ client account management?

findings.claim: Karpathy's compiled-wiki pattern (2026) treats knowledge sources like a compiler: transform raw source material into denser, structured wiki artifacts via LLM compilation, then query the compiled artifact rather than re-retrieve sources on every query.
findings.evidence: Karpathy's LLM Wiki is a 3-layer architecture — raw sources (preserved), LLM-compiled markdown pages (synthesized), and schema file (index). New data integrates incrementally: extract from source, find existing related pages, append/update/revise as needed. Contrasts with stateless RAG which re-processes from scratch each query. Supports append-only chronological ingest logs capturing what entered, when, from what source, and which pages it touched.

findings.claim: Evergreen note systems and Zettelkasten compounding depend on one-directional information flow and supersession discipline, not bidirectional merge.
findings.evidence: Evergreen notes evolve and contribute progressively across projects, accumulating into webs of interconnected knowledge where structure emerges from links rather than categorical storage. Compounding happens via forward links and provenance chains — each new note links back to the concept it refines, creating traceable lineage. Zettelkasten's atomic note principle requires each note to contain one idea with explicit connections; this works via one-directional forward references, not merged synthesis.

findings.claim: LLM bidirectional merging of prose documents (the approach Jack uses for syncing GDrive ↔ MM2) introduces three documented data-loss and fabrication risks: (1) semantic drift from repeated summarization, (2) conflict resolution bias toward generated content over retrieved truth, (3) hallucinated citations and facts during merge.
findings.evidence: Research on 'Blinded by Generated Contexts' (arXiv 2401.11911) shows LLMs exhibit pronounced bias toward generated contexts even when retrieved contexts contain correct answers. Generated text shows higher text-similarity to queries (0.37 vs 0.18 Jaccard), making it seem more relevant. When semantic integrity is broken (sentence-level truncation in retrieved passages), the bias worsens. As LLM-generated content proliferates, retrieval systems risk returning AI-produced hallucinations to LLMs, which preferentially rely on them. Researchers warn this creates 'susceptibility to misinformation.' Separately, merge operations on finetuned LLMs show factuality drops (92.7–94.4%) and information loss from parameter conflicts.

findings.claim: Evolving memory systems in multi-agent LLM deployments accumulate errors: memory drift (semantic corruption over time), implicit conflicts (fragments appear similar but differ semantically), and cumulative errors (unlike static RAG where errors isolate, errors in evolving memory persist across future queries).
findings.evidence: Survey on 'Governing Evolving Memory in LLM Agents' (arXiv 2603.11768) identifies 'agent drift' as a primary failure mode where behavioral degradation occurs when memory evolves unpredictably. Agents may distort facts through repeated summarization (semantic drift) or reinforce suboptimal workflows. Unlike static RAG systems where errors are isolated, errors in evolving memory are cumulative and persistent. The paper proposes SSGM (Stability and Safety Governed Memory) framework requiring: (1) structured memory protocols with standardized formats, (2) regular verification audits, (3) access controls restricting modification, (4) temporal constraints managing evolution speed.

findings.claim: Single-writer-multiple-reader architecture (AM Brain's model) prevents concurrency-driven data loss in shared memory better than bidirectional sync. Multi-agent settings require explicit consistency mechanisms: update-time visibility (when writes become observable) and read-time conflict resolution (how stale artifacts are reconciled).
findings.evidence: Research on multi-agent memory (agent-coherence framework) identifies single-writer pattern as the concurrency solution: only one agent holds write permission at a time; concurrent writes prevented at protocol level, not resolved post-hoc by merge. When systems write all information into shared pools without separation, consistency conflicts emerge: different agents write contradictory facts, outdated info persists, misleading reasoning results. Approaches for multi-agent memory include: (1) central memory with locking, (2) namespaces (each agent writes only to its own space), (3) append-only logs (resolve conflicts at read time). The single-writer avoids all three failure modes.

findings.claim: Narrative accumulation (Jack's narrative.md dual-load) as a durable memory layer is evidence-supported for longitudinal account understanding, but when combined with bidirectional sync, the narrative layer becomes a hallucination sink.
findings.evidence: Amory framework (arXiv 2601.06282) shows narrative-driven memory architecture organizes agent interactions into coherent story structures that compress long histories while preserving semantic relationships. Cross-Session Narrative Memory (CSNM) reduces context token costs ~40x while maintaining longitudinal coherence through semantic compression. This works for single-writer systems where one agent manages the narrative consolidation. However, when narratives are bidirectionally synced (GDrive ↔ MM2), each sync window creates merge opportunities where LLMs preferentially select generated over retrieved content; narrative embeds that preference permanently.

findings.claim: Backup vs sync distinction: one-way backup (AM Brain REV3 GDrive → git copy, never synced back) is structurally safer than bidirectional sync for knowledge systems. Bidirectional sync can catastrophically propagate deletions, corruptions, and conflicts. One-way backup preserves history and recovery paths.
findings.evidence: Industry consensus on backup vs sync: (1) one-way backup copies files without altering source; deletions on one side do not cascade. (2) Bidirectional sync keeps locations matched; deletion on one side syncs everywhere, removing recovery paths. For knowledge management, second-brain systems linking related ideas can suffer 'if someone accidentally deletes a critical contract, that deletion syncs everywhere, leaving no way to recover it.' AM Brain's one-way backup model retains git history and never propagates deletions back from the backup layer.

findings.claim: AM Brain's two-tier (mirror + authored) + one-way backup model aligns with research evidence on memory durability and compounding. Jack's bidirectional merge model trades data durability for operational convenience but incurs documented hallucination and conflict risks.
findings.evidence: AM Brain design separates MIRROR tier (thin, regenerable caches; fetched_at; freely deletable) from AUTHORED tier (durable truth; git-versioned; field-level ownership). This two-tier separation matches research on 'canonical memory' vs 'derived indexes' from multiple agentic-memory papers. Canonical stores must remain single-writer; derived caches can be regenerated. One-way backup to GDrive preserves history without bidirectional propagation risk. Jack's approach treats GDrive + MM2 as equivalent peers with 30-minute bidirectional sync and LLM merge, introducing all documented merge risks. REV3 explicitly rejected this: 'bidirectional prose-merge silently loses + fabricates data; a hosted scheduler can't natively touch raw-.md+git.'

findings.claim: Supersession discipline (newer source weakens older claim automatically) is essential for compounding knowledge in evolving systems; requires one-directional flow to implement cleanly.
findings.evidence: Karpathy's LLM Wiki pattern explicitly includes 'lifecycle features including confidence scoring, supersession, and basic retention decay to prevent the wiki from becoming a junk drawer. A claim from a newer source should weaken an older one automatically.' This requires a canonical, version-ordered append-log. Bidirectional merge cannot enforce supersession cleanly: when GDrive and MM2 both modify the same claim with different confidence scores, conflict resolution (which wins?) must default to a rule. AM Brain's one-way backup + git history naturally preserve timestamps and authorship.

findings.claim: Citation-based accountability and provenance chains are foundational for AM systems managing 30+ accounts. Single-canonical-store architecture (AM Brain) preserves provenance; bidirectional merge (Jack) breaks trace-back chains when conflicts are resolved.
findings.evidence: AM Brain principle I-4: 'Mirror the system of record; never invent its keys.' Every field carries source/fetched_at; every recommendation carries score_reason + confidence. One-way flow preserves these chains. Jack's sync uses GDrive-wins-on-conflict rule, but when Metamate writes narrative.md to MM2 and Claude Code updates it on GDrive, the sync's LLM merge may blend both versions, losing the original Metamate timestamp and context. The 164-silent-run disaster Jack's doc mentions was caused by 'personal+bidirectional+silent' — even with GDrive-wins rule, the blending loses intermediate states.

recommended_patterns: ONE CANONICAL STORE (not synced replicas): Designate a single source of truth (AM Brain's Metamate project memory) with one writer at a time per file. Read-only mirrors (GDrive backup) are acceptable for DR and history; never edit or sync them back.

APPEND-ONLY INGEST LOGS: Maintain chronological record of what entered the system, when, from what source, and which knowledge pages it touched. Enables supersession discipline, audit trails, and recovery without merge conflicts.

TWO-TIER MEMORY SEPARATION: Durable AUTHORED tier (git-versioned, field-level ownership, human-confirmed) separate from MIRROR tier (regenerable caches, freely deletable, auto-built). Prevents over-merging of transient state with durable truth.

NARRATIVE ACCUMULATION + SINGLE WRITER: Adopt Jack's narrative.md longitudinal meeting log and Amory's narrative-binding consolidation, BUT feed a single narrative writer (not bidirectional sync). The writer merges sources (CRM, transcripts, emails) into a coherent narrative; Metamate agents and Claude Code read the same narrative, never both write.

STRUCTURED MEMORY PROTOCOLS + GOVERNANCE (SSGM pattern): Implement standardized formats for memory storage, regular verification audits, access controls restricting modification, and temporal constraints managing evolution speed. Required to prevent semantic drift in evolving 30-account systems.

SINGLE-WRITER-MULTIPLE-READER per file/artifact: Enforce at protocol level. Use file-level locking (AM Brain's /memories/project/<id>/ single-owner frontmatter) or transaction semantics (MM2 eventual consistency + owner fields). Prevents concurrent-write conflicts.

SUPERSESSION + CONFIDENCE SCORING: Every synthesized claim carries source_date, freshness_shelf_life, confidence (high|medium|low), and supersession rule ('newer source weakens older claim'). Implement at ingest time, not query time, to avoid merge re-deciding priority.

ONE-WAY BACKUP FOR DURABILITY: Use git on the canonical store for version history and recovery. Use one-way exports to secondary storage (GDrive) for DR and human browsing. NEVER edit secondary or sync back. This preserves deletion recovery and audit trail without bidirectional propagation risk.

CITATION + PROVENANCE per claim: Every recommendation must link to source signal (with timestamp), evidence object (with clearance class), and reasoning. One-canonical-store preserves these chains; bidirectional merge breaks them when conflicts are auto-resolved.

CONFLICT PREVENTION via OWNERSHIP not RESOLUTION: Assign field-level ownership rules (I-5 in AM Brain): each section is human-owned, agent-suggests (under suggested:), or generated. Do NOT auto-merge conflicting versions; instead, block the merge and flag to the human owner. Jack's 30-min sync + auto-merge is the anti-pattern.
pkm-compounding.implications: FOR AM BRAIN STORAGE/MEMORY/RUNTIME DECISIONS: (1) REV3 single-store decision is EVIDENCE-SUPPORTED: AM Brain's flip from GDrive+git-canonical to Metamate project memory canonical (with one-way GDrive backup) aligns with Karpathy's 2026 compiled-wiki pattern, research on evolving-memory systems requiring single-writer governance, Zettelkasten/evergreen-notes compounding via one-directional flow. Jack's bidirectional merge introduces all documented hallucination/loss risks. (2) HYBRID LAYER (Jack's strengths + AM Brain's structure) IS VIABLE: Adopt Jack's narrative.md accumulation + Amory's narrative-binding + CRM-notes-ingestion, BUT feed through single narrative-writer (Metamate mission or Claude Code skill writing once per 12h sweep, never bidirectional). Preserves Jack's longitudinal coherence without sync risks. AM Brain already does this (§7, §11). (3) GOVERNANCE STACK BEFORE AUTONOMY SCALE: Before expanding from 1 AM to multi-AM or subagents, implement SSGM-style governance: structured memory protocols, verification audits, access controls, temporal evolution constraints. Jack's 34-account system is mature; scaling to 50+ accounts + bidirectional merge + 5 agents is untested. (4) APPENDIX-ONLY INGEST LOG IS LOW-COST, HIGH-LEVERAGE: Both systems should maintain explicit append-only records of 'what entered when from what source → touched which pages.' Enables supersession discipline, rollback, audit trails. Neither documents this explicitly; add before Phase 2 autonomy. (5) MEMORY DRIFT + SEMANTIC CORRUPTION GROWS WITH SCALE: Evolving-memory systems accumulate errors (semantic drift, implicit conflicts, cumulative errors). Jack's 34-account + bidirectional-sync may already experience this silently. At 33 APAC accounts with potential subagents, build in drift detection: (a) store confidence scores + source dates, (b) monthly audit of claim contradictions, (c) quarterly new-source vs old-claims comparison (supersession log). (6) JACK'S MATURITY IS REAL; REV3'S EXECUTION IS BLOCKER: Jack's system is proven daily for 34 accounts. AM Brain REV3 flips canonical store to Metamate project memory (late-beta); runtime spike found Automations cannot natively touch .md+git, no self-serve persistent devserver, ~23-run silent-stop bug. REV3 deferred to 'option 3 (on-laptop),' losing unattended daily-loop. Before production AM Brain, validate Metamate reliability at 33-account scale and fix Automations silent-stop. Otherwise, fall back to Jack with single narrative writer + one-way backup (hybrid approach)."
caveats: Karpathy's compiled-wiki pattern (April 2026) is very recent; widespread production deployment evidence is limited. AM Brain's application to account management is novel.

Jack's bidirectional-sync system (34 ANZ accounts) is production-proven but operationally undocumented for merge-conflict incident rates. Risk is inferred from research rather than observed in Jack's logs.
Research on evolving-memory semantic drift (SSGM, Amory papers) is 2026 academic work; industry incident data on cumulative errors from bidirectional merge is not publicly available.

Cognition's 'single-writer-multiple-reader' recommendation carries no independent validation; Cognition is a vendor. However, underlying computer-architecture research from multi-agent memory papers converges on same pattern.

AM Brain's two-tier + one-way backup is theoretically sound per research, but runtime verification on Metamate infrastructure (project memory size limits, Collab Files concurrency, Automations reliability) is pending. Late-beta platform with known silent-stop bug.

Citation-based provenance is necessary but not sufficient for safety. Malicious or incorrect source data still flows through; citation is audit trail, not guarantee. Requires upstream data-access audit (Phase -1).

Supersession discipline via 'newer source weakens older claim' requires careful implementation: what if new source is weaker? Recommend explicit operator judgment per domain, not automatic age-based rules.

ai-memory.angle: Agentic / LLM long-term memory architectures 2025-2026: Single canonical store vs bidirectional sync vs hybrid; what does evidence say about AM Brain vs Jack's Second Brain storage decision

findings.claim: Bidirectional sync architectures silently lose data via race conditions and last-write-wins overwrites without business-context awareness
findings.evidence: Stacksync engineering analysis reveals dual-pipeline sync lacks centralized state tracking, creating race conditions where concurrent writes trigger arbitrary last-write-wins behavior; circular update loops are nearly impossible to prevent without coordinated change tracking. Specific failure modes: conflicting updates overwrite each other unpredictably across sync cycles, and probability of race conditions grows exponentially with volume.

findings.claim: Production agentic memory systems in 2026 recommend append-only episodic logs (events) feeding a separate semantic store, not bidirectional merge of prose
findings.evidence: 2026 Enterprise Memory Stack architecture (Alok Mishra): treats episodes as append-only event logs stored in relational/document stores; semantic facts extracted separately via background consolidation. This unidirectional flow (episodic→semantic) avoids simultaneous writes to the same layer. No bidirectional merge; no prose consolidation loops.

findings.claim: Single canonical store (append-only log as source of truth) is the emerging 2026 pattern, not distributed eventual-consistency stores
findings.evidence: Mem0 2026 State of Agent Memory report: memory is now 'a first-class architectural component' with standardized benchmarks. However, this uses user/agent/session/org scopes that compose at retrieval time, not a unified monolithic store. Best-performing systems achieve ~6,900 tokens per query (vs ~26,000 for full-context) through intelligent retrieval across scoped memories. Recent research (ArXiv 2604.20158 'Stateless Decision Memory for Enterprise AI Agents') emphasizes append-only event logs as single source of truth for consistency.

findings.claim: Append-only without aging/pruning creates staleness noise: old versions coexist with new facts, and agents retrieve stale entries with higher confidence due to false precedent
findings.evidence: Oracle Developer Blog + ArXiv 2603.07670: without compression/decay, old and new versions of facts coexist in append-only stores. Agents retrieve old entries with *more* confidence than fresh ones because 'memory had given the wrong answer the appearance of established precedent.' This is 'benign data corruption'—agents reused stale results more confidently than live data. Temporal decay (exponential age-based score multiplier) is required structural solution.

findings.claim: Anthropic Claude API memory tool and Anthropic-documented patterns use client-side filesystem as canonical store, not bidirectional sync with a hosted service
findings.evidence: Claude API memory tool documentation (platform.claude.com): memory tool operates client-side on your own filesystem; you control storage (file, database, cloud storage, encrypted files). No auto-sync back to Anthropic. Multi-session software development pattern (documented in 'Effective harnesses for long-running agents') uses memory files for state recovery across sessions + git-based recovery. No mention of bidirectional merge or hosted memory as canonical.

findings.claim: Claude Code uses three-layer hybrid architecture (memory.md + grep-based live retrieval + Chyros daemon), not bidirectional sync
findings.evidence: MindStudio analysis of Claude Code architecture: persistent memory.md (cross-session, durable), active grep-based retrieval (real-time accuracy), background Chyros daemon (semantic indexing, unshipped). Consistency managed through layered fallback—if memory.md overflows, grep finds live code. No bidirectional merge; memory.md is read/write by Claude but not synced back to a hosted service.

findings.claim: Salesforce enterprise memory for agents uses write-gates + read-gates + confidence scoring, rejecting both silent fabrication and bidirectional merge
findings.evidence: Salesforce Engineering: memory architecture treats memory as 'core platform capability' with explicit write gates (only high-quality candidates), read gates (task-relevant retrieval), confidence scoring (representing uncertainty not false certainty), and source tracking. Multiple agents write to a unified derivation pipeline feeding a single memory store. No bidirectional merge; writes are gated/validated before storage.

findings.claim: Karpathy-pattern compiled wiki (markdown pages that accrue knowledge cross-project, linking back to sources, supersession not decay) is now documented as scalable long-term memory pattern
findings.evidence: 2026 sources on compounding knowledge loop (MindStudio, Towards Data Science) describe Karpathy's LLM wiki pattern: flat typed pages, interlinked, append to pages vs deleting, supersession markers show old facts are outmoded not lost. This differs from both bidirectional merge (no LLM consolidation loops) and pure append-only (explicit supersession prevents staleness noise).

findings.claim: Multi-agent systems require strong consistency (ACID atomicity) for shared memory, not eventual consistency; single write-owner per-file pattern is Meta-endorsed over bidirectional merge
findings.evidence: O'Reilly 'Why Multi-Agent Systems Need Memory Engineering' + ArXiv 2603.10062: shared memory requires atomic updates (all succeed or all fail); eventual consistency is only acceptable for agent-scoped working memory. Recent production systems (including Meta's internal patterns cited in AM Brain canonical) use per-file single-writer model over distributed eventual-consistency.

findings.claim: In 2026, the hard problem is deciding what to remember and preventing retrieval from 'quietly lying,' not choosing storage backend
findings.evidence: ByteRover + research summary (blog.bymar.co): five distinct architectural camps exist (raw preservation, profile extraction, reflective synthesis, hierarchical context, version-controlled knowledge). All solve the same core problem differently: selective ingestion, update semantics for contradictions/drift, multi-signal retrieval beyond semantic similarity, meaningful compression, and observable failure modes. No single storage architecture is universal.

recommended_patterns: [synth] Single canonical store (append-only episode log) with asynchronous, unidirectional consolidation to semantic/authored layers. This matches both AM Brain REV3 (Metamate MM2 as canonical) and production patterns (Salesforce, Mem0, Anthropic).
[synth] Per-file single-writer discipline (not per-AM, per-file) for shared memory—matches Meta-endorsed pattern cited in AM Brain I-5 and avoids bidirectional merge entirely.

[synth] Two-tier dossier (mirror caches with fetched_at + authored truth with git versioning) is supported by evidence: episodic/mirror tier for regenerable facts; authored tier for durable synthesis. AM Brain's design here is architecturally sound.

[synth] Append-only with explicit supersession markers (not decay functions or versioning) prevents staleness noise while preserving audit trail. Evidence: Karpathy wiki pattern + AM Brain's 'supersession > decay' principle is aligned with research.

[synth] Write gates before canonical storage—validate data quality/confidence before writing, not after retrieval. Salesforce pattern; prevents 'benign data corruption' where agents reuse stale facts with false confidence.

[Jack] Reject bidirectional sync (LLM-merge + 30-min cycle + GDrive↔MM2) as canonical architecture. Evidence shows last-write-wins races, no conflict resolution with business context, and circular update loops. Jack's production system documented 'GDrive wins on conflict' but evidence says this is a latent data-loss pattern.

[AM/ext] Metamate MM2 as canonical is operationally sound *if* backed by mandatory backup (DR), per-file single-writer, and strict no-sync-back rule (markdown+git = read-only export, never synced back). AM Brain REV3 design doc confirms this; evidence supports it.

[AM/ext] If considering hosted-scheduler-compatible design (AM Brain's option 3: on-demand on laptop now, devserver later), validate file I/O is native or use append-only event API (not LLM-merge-in-the-mission), and enforce single-writer per file at the host level.

[synth] Hybrid: one canonical store + one-way backup + optional retrieval index (para-learn FAISS/BM25). Do NOT add a second live store, second LLM-merge loop, or bidirectional sync. Evidence: 100% of production systems avoid bidirectional merge.

[ext] Temporal decay (staleness scoring) is optional optimization layer *after* mandatory supersession. Evidence shows decay alone leaves stale facts in circulation; mark-as-outmoded (supersession) is the deterministic control.
ai-memory.implications: For AM Brain storage/memory/runtime decisions: (1) REV3's flip to Metamate MM2 as canonical is evidence-backed—single canonical store with one-way backup is the 2026 consensus pattern, not bidirectional merge. Jack's production system (GDrive↔MM2 sync + LLM-merge) represents a latent architecture that works operationally but violates evidence-based principles: bidirectional sync causes silent data loss via race conditions, LLM prose-merge contradicts Salesforce/Anthropic discipline (write gates before storage, not merge-after-retrieval), and eventual-consistency is only acceptable for agent-scoped memory, not shared account-manager knowledge. (2) AM Brain's two-tier dossier (mirror+authored) is sound and research-aligned; the key is maintaining strict directionality (mirror→authored, never back) and enforcing single-writer-per-file. (3) The runtime-host question (devserver vs Metamate Automations vs laptop) is orthogonal to storage architecture: the evidence says canonical store must be client-side-controllable (filesystem, your DB, your memory API), not dependent on the host's native memory platform. Metamate MM2 works because MM2 project-memory is accessible to both operator (Collab Files) and cron (Automations), avoiding the git-in-mount / devserver-provisioning trap. (4) Append-only-with-supersession is the correct staleness pattern; temporal decay (Alok Mishra's exponential score multiplier) is a read-side optimization, not a substitute for explicit "this fact is outmoded" marking. (5) Jack's skill-based multi-agent fleet (14 skills + Metamate + Claude Code + MyClaw) is operationally mature and valuable; the architectural mistake is the bidirectional sync + dual-lock mechanism, not the skill-layer or the enriched registry. Hybrid path: adopt Jack's skills + registry enrichment + narrative.md dual-load + CRM-note ingestion, but keep storage as single canonical (AM Brain REV3 or a modified version with Jack's per-skill write ownership mapped onto AM Brain's per-file ownership).
caveats: None of the 2025-2026 research explicitly modeled the 33-account AM use case (Jack's 34 accounts is closest). The evidence generalizes from enterprise memory systems (Salesforce millions of users, Mem0 multi-tenant, Claude Managed Agents), so extrapolation to a single-operator AM is reasonable but not 1:1 validated.

Anthropic's Claude Code memory.md architecture is documented for code-centric workflows (repo navigation, session recovery); the evidence for how well it scales to domain knowledge (account strategies, client relationships) is indirect (no published case study of a long-running account-manager using Claude Code memory).

Salesforce's write-gates + read-gates system is described at a high level; the actual gate algorithms (what triggers rejection, what confidence threshold prevents storage) are not public. AM Brain's principle of 'Unknown ≠ zero' aligns with Salesforce's confidence-scoring, but exact implementation details differ.

The Mem0 multi-scope architecture (user/agent/session/org-level memories composing at retrieval) is flexible but introduces complexity: a single-operator system like AM Brain would likely collapse org-scope to operator-scope, simplifying the model. Evidence doesn't address whether this simplification preserves the performance benefits (6,900 tokens vs 26,000).

Bidirectional sync failures (race conditions, last-write-wins loss) are well-documented for distributed systems (Stacksync), but the empirical data on *how often* these occur in a low-frequency (daily cockpit, <10 writes/day) system like Jack's is absent. The 164-silent-run disaster cited in AM Brain REV3 (personal+bidirectional+silent) is anecdotal evidence, not a systematic study.

Karpathy wiki / compounding knowledge loop is presented as a pattern but lacks published benchmarks on staleness/freshness compared to pure append-only or LLM-merge approaches. AM Brain's 'supersession > decay' design is untested in production.
